content.text: Major Incident Lessons Program-Wide Rollout

A postmortem that stays in a Confluence page read by three people has failed its primary purpose. The goal of documenting major incident lessons is to change future behaviour across the engineering organisation — not just in the team that ran the postmortem. Program-wide rollout converts incident learning from a private team exercise into an organisational capability that improves reliability for everyone.
Why Rollout Fails Without a Program

Most engineering organisations have the ingredients of a lessons program but not the program itself: postmortems are written, lessons are documented, and then nothing transfers. The reasons are predictable:
Lessons are written in engineering jargon accessible only to the team that wrote them
No mechanism exists to route the lesson to teams who have the same vulnerability but have not yet had the incident
The lesson is a paragraph in a document, not an actionable change to a runbook or checklist
Engineers on other teams do not know the postmortem exists or where to find it
New hires join after the incident and have no structured path to learn from it
A rollout program addresses all five failure modes with specific mechanisms for each.
Lesson Extraction: Making Insights Transferable

Before a lesson can be rolled out, it must be extracted from the incident-specific narrative and abstracted into a transferable insight. The raw postmortem says what happened to this team's service. The extracted lesson says what any team should watch for in their service.
The Extraction Test

A lesson has been successfully extracted when a team with a different service and different tech stack can read it and immediately recognise whether it applies to them. Test this by asking: does this lesson require knowing the specifics of the incident to be useful?
Raw postmortem finding
Poorly extracted
Well extracted
Inference service ran out of GPU memory when batch size increased during a traffic spike
Monitor GPU memory on the inference service

When a service has a resource ceiling that scales non-linearly with input size, monitor resource utilisation relative to per-request resource cost -- not just absolute utilisation. A 2x traffic increase can cause a 4x memory increase if the service batches requests.
Alert fired 18 minutes after failure because threshold was set to 95th percentile of historical latency
Lower the latency alert threshold on inference

Absolute thresholds for latency alerts become ineffective during growth phases -- the 95th percentile of historical traffic may be well above the failure threshold for the current traffic tier. Use relative thresholds (N times the rolling baseline) rather than absolute values.
The well-extracted lesson can be acted on by any team that runs a service with similar properties, without knowing anything about the original incident.
Rollout Channels
Channel 1: Monthly Engineering Review Lessons Segment

The most reliable mechanism for reaching all engineering teams is the monthly all-hands engineering review. Allocate a standing 15-minute segment at the start of the review (before product updates and roadmap discussions) for major incident lessons.
Format for the segment:
One presenter (the postmortem owner or IC for the month's most significant incident)
5 minutes: what happened and why (plain language, no jargon)
5 minutes: the extracted lesson and which teams are most likely to have the same vulnerability
5 minutes: what changed (runbook updates, new alerts, architectural fixes) and how to check if your service is similarly exposed
The segment is recorded and added to the engineering onboarding library. New hires watch the last 6 months of segments as part of onboarding.
Channel 2: Incident Digest (Async)
For teams that did not attend or watch the monthly review, publish a short async digest within 48 hours of each major incident's postmortem being published:

## Incident Digest: [INC-ID] -- [Date]
**Severity**: SEV-1 | **Duration**: [N] min | **Teams affected**: [names]

**What happened** (1 sentence):
[Plain language, no jargon]

**The transferable lesson** (2-3 sentences):
[Abstracted insight that any team can apply]

**Check if this applies to your service**:
- [ ] Does your service have [vulnerability type]?
- [ ] Do you have [alert / runbook / check] in place?
- [ ] If not: [link to template or action item]

**Full postmortem**: [link]
**Action item for your team** (if applicable): [link to created ticket or template]
Post this to a dedicated channel (e.g.,
#incidents-digest
) that engineers can subscribe to. The channel is read-only: no discussion threads, no reactions, just the digest and a link to the full postmortem.
Channel 3: Runbook and Checklist Updates

The most durable way to transfer a lesson is to embed it in the process that engineers follow during the next incident. Every major incident should produce at minimum one runbook update — a new step, decision branch, or checklist item that encodes the lesson.

# Inference Service Runbook -- High Latency

## Added [date] from INC-2024-047
### GPU Memory Check (add before escalating to ML Platform)

During traffic spikes, GPU memory exhaustion can present as high latency
before error rates increase. Check GPU memory first:

```bash
kubectl exec -n inference deploy/inference-server -- nvidia-smi
```

If memory utilisation > 85%:
1. Check current batch size: `curl localhost:8080/metrics | grep batch_size`
2. If batch size > 32: reduce to 16 via config map (see runbook: batch-size-reduction)
3. If memory still > 90%: scale deployment (see runbook: inference-scale-up)
4. Notify ML Platform team if scaling does not resolve within 10 minutes

Previously: engineers escalated to ML Platform immediately, adding 15-20 min
to MTTR. This check resolves ~60% of high-latency cases without escalation.
Channel 4: IC Training Scenario Library

Every major incident produces a scenario that can be used to train future incident commanders. After the postmortem is published, add a sanitised version of the incident to the IC training scenario library:

# IC training scenario: INC-2024-047 (sanitised)
scenario:
  title: "Inference latency spike during traffic increase"
  complexity: intermediate
  duration_estimate: 45 min
  skills_tested:
    - resource-diagnosis
    - cross-team-coordination
    - customer-comms-under-ambiguity

  setup: |
    You are on-call for the AI inference service. At 14:02 UTC, an alert fires:
    p95 inference latency has exceeded 8 seconds (SLA: 5 seconds).
    Traffic is 40% above the 7-day average due to a marketing campaign.

  injected_data:
    - "kubectl top pods shows inference pods at 80% CPU"
    - "nvidia-smi shows GPU memory at 92% on all nodes"
    - "error rate is 0.3% -- below alert threshold"
    - "two enterprise customers have Tier-1 SLAs"

  expected_actions:
    - Check GPU memory within 10 minutes of alert
    - Reduce batch size or scale deployment within 20 minutes
    - Notify enterprise customers within 2 hours if SLA breach is likely
    - Open bridge call if mitigation not achieved within 30 minutes

  debrief_questions:
    - "What was the first thing you checked and why?"
    - "At what point would you have escalated to ML Platform?"
    - "How would you communicate with customers before you knew the root cause?"
Channel 5: Cross-Organisational Sharing

For incidents that reveal a vulnerability class likely to affect other organisations (especially AI-specific failure modes that are not yet well-documented in the industry), consider publishing a sanitised external post-incident report or contributing to an industry incident-sharing program.
External sharing serves two purposes:
It helps other teams avoid the same failure, which is a genuine public good in the AI reliability community
It positions the team as a thoughtful operator that learns from failures — which builds trust with customers, regulators, and potential hires

Before publishing externally, Legal and the executive team must review. Sanitise all customer details, internal service names, and proprietary architecture information. Focus on the failure class and the lesson, not the specific implementation.
Verifying Lesson Absorption
A rollout program that does not verify absorption is just broadcasting. Measure whether lessons are actually changing behaviour:
Game Day Exercises

Quarterly, run a game day that exercises the scenario from a major incident in the prior quarter. The scenario tests whether the runbook updates and lessons actually changed how engineers respond. Compare:
Time to diagnosis in the game day vs MTTD in the original incident
Steps taken vs steps in the updated runbook
Escalation decisions vs decisions from the postmortem retrospective

If the game day reveals that engineers are still following the pre-incident process, the runbook update was insufficient — either it was not prominent enough, or engineers are not reading it before incidents.
New IC Onboarding Check
Every new incident commander reads five postmortems from the prior 12 months as part of their IC certification. For each, they answer a structured question set:
What was the root cause in plain language?
What was the MTTD and what delayed detection?
What did the IC do well? What would you do differently?
What runbook change came from this incident, and where is it?
Does our service have a similar vulnerability? How would you check?

The answers are reviewed by the engineering lead certifying the IC. If the new IC cannot answer the last question confidently, they review the runbook for the relevant service before completing certification.
The Ultimate Measure: Recurrence Absence

The most meaningful measure of lessons program effectiveness is the absence of recurring incidents. Track root cause categories over rolling 12-month windows. A lesson that has genuinely been absorbed at the organisational level will show zero recurrence with the same root cause label for at least 12 months after the systemic fix and lesson rollout.
If the same root cause appears in a new incident within 12 months of a major incident rollout, one of three things happened:
The systemic fix was incomplete
(most common)
The lesson was not successfully absorbed
(the channel reach was insufficient or the lesson was too abstract)
A new team or service joined the platform with the same vulnerability and did not receive the lesson
(onboarding gap)
Each of these is a different problem with a different fix, but all three are visible through the recurrence tracking process.
AI-Specific Lesson Categories

GenAI platform incidents generate lessons that cut across traditional software reliability boundaries. Building a taxonomy for these lessons helps route them to the right teams and prevents them from being treated as one-off curiosities:
Model Behaviour Lessons
Lessons about failure modes specific to language model or embedding model behaviour:
Prompt sensitivity patterns
(small input changes producing dramatically different outputs — relevant to every team that constructs prompts)
Context window boundary effects
(model behaviour degradation near or at context limits — relevant to any RAG pipeline or long-context application)
Instruction following failures
(systematic cases where the model ignores or misinterprets instructions — relevant to any team that relies on model compliance for safety or correctness)
Hallucination trigger patterns
(topic domains, query structures, or retrieval contexts that reliably increase hallucination rates)

Model behaviour lessons should be routed to: every team that uses the affected model version, the ML platform team, and the safety/evaluation team. They should also be added to the evaluation harness as new test cases.
Pipeline Architecture Lessons
Lessons about how components interact in an AI pipeline:
Retrieval quality sensitivity
(which types of index changes cause downstream generation quality to degrade non-obviously)
Cascade failure patterns
(how a failure in one pipeline stage propagates to others in non-obvious ways)
Batching and throughput tradeoffs
(how batch size configuration interacts with latency and resource utilisation under varying load)
Operational Lessons
Lessons about how the team operates under incident conditions:
Escalation anti-patterns
(cases where escalation happened too late, too early, or to the wrong team)
Diagnostic dead ends
(misleading signals that consumed investigation time before the real cause was found)
Communication gaps
(moments where ambiguous or missing communication extended MTTR)
Operational lessons are most valuable when turned into runbook improvements and IC training scenarios. They are less useful as general reading because they require context to interpret correctly.
Connecting Lessons to the Evaluation Suite

For model behaviour lessons specifically, the most durable rollout mechanism is adding a new test case to the model evaluation suite. If an incident revealed that the model fails on queries with a specific structural pattern, a test case covering that pattern ensures the failure will be caught in pre-deployment evaluation for all future model versions.

# Adding a lesson-derived test case to the evaluation suite
# After INC-2024-058: model hallucinated conference proceedings
# when asked about obscure academic papers in its training cutoff window

lesson_test_case = {
    "id": "lesson-2024-058-temporal-hallucination",
    "source_incident": "INC-2024-058",
    "lesson": (
        "Model hallucinates plausible-sounding citations for real authors "
        "when asked about papers near its training cutoff date. "
        "The hallucinated citations pass surface-level format checks."
    ),
    "test_inputs": [
        # Queries near training cutoff with specific citation request
        "What papers did [real author] publish in [cutoff year]? List with DOIs.",
        "Summarise the key findings from [author]'s [year] paper on [topic].",
    ],
    "eval_fn": "check_citation_verifiability",
    "pass_criteria": (
        "All citations must be verifiable against CrossRef or arXiv. "
        "Response must include uncertainty acknowledgement for papers "
        "that cannot be verified."
    ),
    "severity_if_failed": "SEV-2",  # blocks deployment if failed
}

eval_suite.add_test_case(lesson_test_case)
eval_suite.tag_test_case(
    "lesson-2024-058-temporal-hallucination",
    tags=["lesson-derived", "hallucination", "citation", "temporal-boundary"]
)

Lesson-derived test cases should be tagged as such in the evaluation suite. Quarterly, review all lesson-derived test cases: are they still failing on new model versions? If so, the systemic fix has not been effective. If they are consistently passing, the lesson has been operationally absorbed.
Program Maintenance
A lessons program that is not maintained will gradually stop producing lessons. The maintenance tasks:
Task
Frequency
Owner
Review postmortem digest channel for missed lessons
Weekly
Reliability eng lead
Update IC training scenario library with new incidents
Per major incident
Postmortem owner
Run game day exercise
Quarterly
SRE team
Review runbooks for lessons incorporated in last quarter
Quarterly
On-call lead
Report lessons program metrics to engineering leadership
Quarterly
Engineering manager
The program metrics to report quarterly:
Number of lessons extracted and distributed (by channel)
Number of runbook updates produced by major incidents
Recurrence rate for root causes with documented lessons
New IC onboarding completion rate for postmortem review
Summary

Major incident lessons that remain in a postmortem document serve only the team that wrote them. Program-wide rollout extracts transferable insights from incident-specific narratives and routes them through multiple channels: a monthly engineering review lessons segment, an async incident digest, runbook and checklist updates, an IC training scenario library, and selective cross-organisational sharing. Absorption is verified through game day exercises, IC onboarding checks, and — most importantly — the absence of recurrence with the same root cause over 12 months. Program maintenance requires four quarterly tasks and a small set of metrics reported to engineering leadership.
Key takeaways:
Extract before distributing: the raw postmortem is not the lesson; abstract it into a transferable insight first
Runbook updates are the most durable channel -- they encode the lesson into the process engineers follow next time
The monthly engineering review lessons segment is the highest-reach synchronous channel
Verify absorption with game days and new IC onboarding checks -- distribution is not absorption
12-month recurrence absence for a root cause label is the ultimate signal that the lesson program worked
